era_context: The early Han Dynasty under Emperor Wen is consolidating power. In the Mediterranean, the Roman Republic is in the midst of the Second Macedonian War (200–196 BCE) against Philip V of Macedon. The Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III is expanding aggressively in the Eastern Mediterranean and Asia Minor. Egypt under Ptolemy V is weakening amid internal instability and regency struggles. The Fourth Syrian War (197–194 BCE) between the Seleucids and Ptolemies is ongoing.

events.title: Battle of Cynoscephalae: Roman victory over Macedon
events.description: The Roman general Titus Quinctius Flamininus defeats Philip V of Macedon at the Battle of Cynoscephalae in Thessaly. This decisive victory effectively ends the Second Macedonian War (200–196 BCE) and establishes Rome as the dominant military power in the Eastern Mediterranean. The Roman legions, with their superior tactical flexibility, overcome the Macedonian phalanx, demonstrating the superiority of Roman military organization. The victory has profound geopolitical consequences: it breaks Macedonian power, isolates the Seleucid Empire, and establishes Rome as the arbiter of Eastern Mediterranean affairs.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Cynoscephalae in 197 BCE is one of the best-attested military engagements of antiquity, recorded in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: Leads to -196-001 (Flamininus proclaims Greek freedom at Isthmian Games)

events.title: Fourth Syrian War: Antiochus III campaigns in Coele-Syria
events.description: Antiochus III of the Seleucid Empire launches a major military campaign to seize Coele-Syria (the region between the Lebanon and Anti-Lebanon mountains) and Phoenician cities from the Ptolemaic Kingdom. This campaign is part of the Fourth Syrian War (197–194 BCE), a conflict between the two great Hellenistic powers over control of the Eastern Mediterranean. Antiochus's campaign is initially successful, bringing much of the region under Seleucid control. However, the campaign will eventually provoke Roman intervention and contribute to the Roman-Syrian War (192–188 BCE).
key_figures: Ptolemy V (Ptolemaic King, nominal ruler; actual power held by regents)
events.certainty_note: The Fourth Syrian War is well-attested in Hellenistic historiography, though exact dates and the sequence of campaigns vary slightly across sources. The campaign in 197 BCE is probable but not definitively dated to this specific year.
cross_references: Part of Fourth Syrian War (197–194 BCE)

events.title: Han Dynasty: Rebellion of the Seven Kings begins
events.description: The Rebellion of the Seven Kings (also known as the Seven States Rebellion) begins in 154 BCE, not 197 BCE. This is a correction: there is no major rebellion in 197 BCE. Instead, Emperor Wen of Han continues his policy of gradual centralization and reduction of regional kingdom autonomy. The consolidation of Han power proceeds through administrative reform rather than military conflict in this period.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, chapters on Han Dynasty

events.certainty_note: No major military or political event is specifically recorded for 197 BCE in Han sources. The period is characterized by gradual administrative consolidation rather than dramatic events.
cross_references: Part of broader Han centralization process (180–157 BCE)

events.title: Ptolemy V's regency: Internal instability in Egypt
events.description: Ptolemy V Epiphanes, the young Ptolemaic king, is still under regency, with real power held by court factions and regents. Egypt is experiencing internal instability, including court intrigues, financial difficulties, and loss of territory to the Seleucid Empire. The regency period (197–180 BCE) is characterized by weak central authority and vulnerability to external pressure, particularly from Antiochus III. This internal weakness contributes to Egypt's loss of Coele-Syria and other territories to the Seleucids.
events.certainty_note: The internal instability of Egypt during Ptolemy V's regency is well-attested in Hellenistic sources, though specific events in 197 BCE are not always clearly dated.
cross_references: Part of broader Ptolemaic decline (197–180 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Cynoscephalae is sometimes portrayed as a decisive triumph of Roman military superiority over the Macedonian phalanx. However, modern scholarship has questioned this narrative: the battle was closely fought, and Macedonian forces were not decisively inferior. The victory owed much to superior Roman generalship (Flamininus) and favorable terrain rather than inherent Roman military superiority. Additionally, the Fourth Syrian War's dating and sequence of campaigns are debated; some scholars argue that Antiochus III's campaigns in Coele-Syria occurred in 195 BCE rather than 197 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 197 BCE is dominated by Greco-Roman sources (Livy, Polybius, Plutarch), which privilege Mediterranean and Near Eastern events. Chinese sources (Shiji, Han Shu) provide good documentation of Han Dynasty affairs but are compiled centuries after the events. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: The Roman victory at Cynoscephalae in 197 BCE enabled Flamininus to proclaim Greek freedom at the Isthmian Games in 196 BCE.
Antiochus III's campaigns in Coele-Syria in 197 BCE (or 195 BCE) provoked Roman intervention, leading to the Roman-Syrian War.
